title: Needle-felted goldfinch
subtitle: A small striking bird with a red face, gold wing bar, and black-and-white head
excerpt: Needle-felt a compact goldfinch in merino roving, capturing the distinctive red face mask, yellow wing flash, and streaked buff breast. The finished bird is about 5 cm long and works well as a seasonal decoration or framed display piece.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. The goldfinch is among the most recognisable British garden birds and a well-established needle-felting subject at guild level.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method in which barbed needles tangle and compact wool fibres without heat or water.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon; the standard starting material for needle-felting projects.

content.text: The goldfinch has five bold colour zones: a red face, white cheeks, a buff breast, black cap and wings, and a yellow wing bar. Work each zone in thin wisps of
. Use a size-40 needle for precise edges.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, buff or warm brown (breast, back)

content.text: Roll about 10 g of mixed roving into a firm oval about 5 cm long and 2.5 cm in diameter. Stab with the size-38 needle, rotating frequently. The body is slightly fuller in the breast area than at the tail end.
Step 2: Apply buff and white base layers

Cover the breast in buff roving and the lower belly in white. Cover the back in a slightly darker buff. Apply thin layers and stab firmly. These form the neutral base over which the patterned colours sit.

The goldfinch head has four distinct zones: a red face forward of the eyes, white cheeks behind the eyes, a black crown on top, and a black chin stripe below. Apply each zone in a small wisp and stab carefully with the size-40 needle. Work boundaries slowly; the contrast between red, white, and black makes the species identifiable.
Step 4: Add the wing and tail markings

Layer a strip of yellow across the centre of each wing area, then add black above and below it. The yellow strip should be about 4 mm wide and run across the full width of the wing zone. Add a small white spot near the wingtip for the wing flash.
Step 5: Add the beak and eyes

Roll a small cone of pale buff roving about 6 mm long. Felt it to a fine point, then attach it to the front of the red face zone. Roll two tiny black balls for the pupils, about 3 mm each, and stab them into the white cheek areas at the eye position.

items.problem: The red face zone bleeds into the white cheeks
items.cause: The roving layer was too thick
items.fix: Remove the offending colour by gently lifting surface fibres with the needle tip and replacing with a fresh thin layer. Always use the size-40 needle at colour boundaries.

items.problem: The yellow wing bar is barely visible
items.cause: The layer was applied too thinly or on the wrong zone of the wing
items.fix: Build the yellow in two or three thin layers. The bar sits across the middle third of the wing.
